summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Undefended hearing – Where the Father and Independent Children’s Lawyer sought final orders be made in the absence of the Mother – Where there is a history of non-attendance and non-compliance by the Mother – Where the Father and Independent Children’s Lawyer sought orders by consent for the Father to have sole decision making responsibility, for the child to live with him, for the Father to be able to obtain an Australian passport for the child without the consent of the Mother, and for the Father to be able to travel internationally with the child without the consent of the Mother – Where the Court made final orders by agreement as between the Father and the Independent Children’s Lawyer and in the absence of the Mother.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

INTRODUCTION

I provide the following short reasons for judgment. I have made orders in relation to a child, X, born in 2020. X is five years old and currently lives with the Applicant Father (“father”) and is not spending time with the Respondent Mother (“mother”). The orders today were made by consent as between the father and the Independent Children’s Lawyer, but in the absence of the mother. The Court is satisfied that in the circumstances of this case, the orders are in the best interest of X.

BACKGROUND

The orders provide that the father have sole decision-making power in relation to all long-term issues, that the child live with the father, and that there are various passport orders. There is a notation to the following effect: that the orders intentionally make no provision for X to spend time with or communicate with the mother, and that any decision about if, when, and how X has future interaction with the mother will be made by the father as an incident of his decision-making power in relation to X.
The matter proceeded on an undefended basis so far as the mother is concerned. The fact that it proceeded on an undefended basis, of course, does not preclude the mother from seeking to re-open the proceedings if she can reasonably satisfy a Court that there is a good explanation for why she could not attend and participate in the proceedings and that she has a plausible case. The absence of the mother today, whilst disappointing, is not surprising. I observe, her absence is entirely consistent with the procedural history of the matter. Even a brief perusal of the record of these proceedings indicates a history of non-attendance by the mother. For example, the mother did not participate when the matter came before the Court on:
4 November 2024;
31 January 2025;
10 February 2025;
14 March 2025;
7 April 2025;
7 August 2025 – during the Child Impact Report interviews, the mother terminated the call prior to the conclusion of the interview; and
1 September 2025, at the Compliance and Readiness Hearing.
The letter which became Exhibit ICL2 – that is the mother’s email of 3 September 2025 – is also evidence that helps to explain why she is not here today. The background of this case is covered quite comprehensively in the reasons for judgment that were given by his Honour Judge Betts on 2 September 2025 at the time that he made orders, suspending all orders in relation to X and ordering that he live with the father and that a recovery order issue.

MATERIAL BEFORE THE COURT

The material before the Court confirms that a recovery order was, in fact, made and that X had to be recovered by the New South Wales Police from the mother’s care and placed into the father’s care. The material before the Court, apart from his Honour’s judgment, includes the Child Impact Report of Court Child Expert, Ms B dated 11 August 2025 (“the Expert”) (“Child Impact Report”). The totality of the materials present a rather sad picture of the mother’s declining mental health and her inability to address the same. At paragraph 31 of the Child Impact Report, for example, the Expert highlights the immediate safety issues for X in the mother’s care. In the circumstances of this case, and even if one looks at it purely and simply from a perspective of safety, there are serious concerns about the mother’s ability to promote the safety of X. The orders made by his Honour and the totality of the evidence before the Court confirms that, at least for the time being, and one would say, based on the mother’s choice not to participate in the proceedings, in the reasonably foreseeable future, X’s safety is best promoted in the care of the father.

DETERMINATION

Experience in this Court indicates that experiencing challenges to a parent’s mental health is not necessarily a disqualifier to being a good parent and having parenting capacity. Experience also indicates that with appropriate treatment, it might be possible for a parent experiencing adverse mental health to re-gain that mental health, such that parenting can be resumed in some form or capacity. I am satisfied, from the father’s evidence, that he is primarily concerned about the best interests of X, that he has the capacity necessary to care for him, both in the short, medium and long-term. I also discerned, from his evidence, a willingness to restore a relationship between X and the mother, subject, first and foremost, to X’s safety. In the circumstances of this case, the making of the orders that I have made on a final basis is very much in the best interests of X.

COSTS OF THE INDEPENDENT CHILDREN’S LAWYER

I will note that in relation to the mother, the usual application for costs of the Independent Children’s Lawyer was made, and the application is declined in the circumstances of this case.
